Page 4: Washington Interscholastic

Washington Interscholastic

There were trophies and cheers for winners, but the fact is everyone won at the first Washington Interscholastic Orienteering Championship held at St. Edward State Park, February 26. The real victor in the season capped by the meet was the sport of orienteering in the Northwest.

The day's glories were shared by teams and individuals from nine different schools and a total of 50 participants on the school course of 2.7 km. (8 controls), designed by Jean Davis. Nathan Hale High School, defeated only once during the regular season, was the runaway team winner, led by Geoff Dillard's outstanding 35:46, some 7 mins. ahead of the next finisher, Brent Benedek of the Cowichan Valley Orienteers (B.C.) and nearly 12 mins. ahead of Marc Olson of Yelm high School. Marc's run was his best of the season, but he had many anxious moments until the control card he dropped after no. 2 was found, preventing disqualification.

Marc's fine run paced Yelm to its best finish of the season, a solid second, and the surprising Blanchet team, a latecomer to the league, was third, paced by John Macaulay's strong 6th place--not bad for a first-time orienteer! Probably the most disappointed team was Lakeside, which had compiled the second best regular-season record. Some of its best orienteers were on a school hike; others were plagued by misfortune--Joe Curren DQ'd, and Woody Rice lost a huge amount of time looking for a control that had disappeared by the time he reached the location.

The consolation for Lakeside was in having the top two high school women, Mara Charnell in first (she was also the season leader) and Kirsten Halvorsen. Third among the H.S. women was Gitte Larsen of Nathan Hale, who surprised her teammate David Ovens (one of the men's leaders all season) and provided valuable points for Hale's team total.

But the star among the women was Maia Halvorsen, the only junior high runner among the women, and the overall woman's winner by nearly two minutes.

What is it? The Prep Page
UW teacher advocating high-school orienteering
by Walt Parietti, Times staff reporter

[Excerpt reprinted from newspaper]
"In jogging, you run for fun and improved health... In cross-country, you have to be a little masochistic at times, often putting up with drenching rain and cold. In orienteering, you become an adventurer. Never certain where you're going and hopeful that you will arrive there first."

...Waugh, a professor at the University of Washington who is vice president of the Cascade Orienteering Club, says: "This is a major international sport, so it's worth trying to promote. I'd like to see some Americans get to the international level. When Americans go over there (to Europe) they don't do anything."

...Hale's Geoff Dillard, who finished first and second in the first two prep meets, explained the competition.
"You have to think a lot more about what you're doing. You don't have to concentrate on doing such a good job of running. It's how you get there instead of how fast you get there."

In Dillard's second orienteering race, "I got messed up really bad on the second control and I got beat by one of my teammates, Dave Ovens, by about seven seconds."

"There is no way to cheat the system by following another runner," Dillard explained. "You have staggered starts about 4 minutes apart. If you run across someone, you don't trust someone else's way any more than you trust your own."...

[Photo Caption: Hale's Geoff Dillard, map in hand, runs past a marker, right, on an orienteering course]

Page 5: Championship

Championship
by Dan Waugh

...Her Collins Academy teammate, Kevin Winston, who at times during the season was his school's lone participant, received a well-deserved trophy for the junior high men.
When we see the persistence and enthusiasm that Kevin, and the second and third place finishers in his division, Ty and Gabe Wingard, display, we know that there is a bright future ahead both for them and for our sport.

We had all been anticipating with great interest the participation of two Canadian school teams. And we were not disappointed. The Cowichan Valley Orienteers from Vancouver Island had the overall second and fifth place finishers, which gave them a solid edge over the OUBC Junior "Deltoids," but not enough to beat Nathan Hale for the international challenge trophy that was awarded.

Nathan Hale received yet another, unexpected award, a trophy donated by Mr. and Mrs. Brock Hogle and named (somewhat embarrassingly) for the undersigned. This impressive cup will be engraved with the winning team's name and will serve as a perpetual trophy for the league. Mr. Hogle, the long-time and extremely successful cross country coach at Nathan Hale H.S., was an early supporter of the league as we began organizing last year, and he kindly agreed to present the awards for this championship. We are most grateful to him for his support.

Not the least of the pleasant surprises for the day was the appearance of a camera crew from KOMO-TV and the KOMO evening news sports commentator, Rick Meeder. They put together a fine little segment on the 6:30 news, which succeeded in giving a good idea of the sport in a concise and very interesting way.

Many thanks are due to others--Mike Schuh and Debbie Newell for having the revised map ready in time, St. Thomas Center for the map sponsorship (and Dave Enger for obtaining the sponsorship), Debbie and Arnold Kay for making the lovely trophies, Jean for her courses, the Millers for registration, Einar Storakers and Harvey Friedman for finish timing, Harry Morgan and Barbara Pedersen for starts, and Nancy Lewis for organizing and contributing to the feeding of hungry orienteers. Many others assisted, whose services if not names, are hereby inscribed for the centuries....

[Sidebar: Saturday, January 8, 1983 - The Seattle Times]
"The whole point," Waugh says, "is to improve their map reading. You use a compass a bit, but as you get better and better you tend to use the compass very little and just read the map. You're moving as fast as you can and at the same time you're studying your map and making strategy decisions."

Strategy on difficult courses involves things like, do you go up the hill or go around? "The more tired you get, the more difficult it is to make this decision," Waugh said.
The winning time for the first high-school event was 17 minutes for 2.4 kilometers. The second was slightly under 33 minutes for 3 kilometers.
But speed of foot does not guarantee success. As an example, Waugh recalled what happened to Hale's best cross-country runner when he competed in his first orienteering meet.
"He ran off not looking carefully at the map," Waugh said. "He took an hour to run a three-kilometer course. That's a very common thing for someone who's a good runner. It takes a while for them to learn the ropes."


Despite Waugh's enthusiasm for the sport, he has had trouble "selling it."
"One of the problems I've had is getting high-school coaches to get their runners to try it," Waugh said. "They feel this will involve a contribution of their time, but that's not necessary. I only want them to encourage their runners to try it."

Brock Hogle, Hale cross country coach, said, "About five or six of our kids do it. It's got a lot of promise. Most of the cross-country kids are pretty smart. You get to use your brains."

What are the chances of the sport's acceptance by the high schools?
Hogle says, "They did something with bowling, so who knows? At least it's something for the kids in the winter. It doesn't involve court time. They just go down and run, check out a map and compass."

Lakeside coach Hugh Tower said, "There are maybe a half dozen of my cross-country kids running. The guy who is handling it is Peter Hayes in our wilderness department. There are quite a few other kids who are not involved in cross country but they do wilderness. They seem to be having an awful lot of fun."

"C.J. Blagg, who's probably my top boy, has been active in it. I think it's fine. It gives them something different to do. I encourage them to keep active. Tom Mills plays basketball. Some are running road runs and marathons. Whatever they do is fine."

[Photo Caption: The Nathan Hale Championship team (l to r): Geoff Dillard, Mike McCool, David Ovens, Dave Steele, and Gitte Larsen, with cross country coach Brock Hogle.]

Winners' Routes
The map shows the routes of Geoff Dillard (G) (solid line), and where different (dashed line), Maia Halvorsen (M). Comments in parentheses are Dan Waugh's.

0-1: G--Read map while running towards trail, so by the time I had to concentrate on the steep terrain I knew where I was going. Looked ahead on map for no. 2. (G and third-place finisher Dave Steele stayed in open area at top of hill as long as possible, instead of taking first trail down.)
1-2: G--Followed trail and used bend as attack point. Checked letter code while running, punched in quickly. (M and second-place finisher Marc Olson used first bend as attack point.)
2-3: M--Tried to cut across but was too low; took me a while to find it. G--Didn't veer up quite enough, so went under control not seeing it (in depression). Knew I had gone too far because of contour lines. (No. 3 was no problem for Dave Steele, who found it directly "by luck" but was nearly Marc Olson's Waterloo, as he went down to trail along lake, overshot, bushwhacked up into green area, and lost his control card.)
3-4: (Here G and M chose the longer way around. Dave and Marc both took other option--dotted line--east to trail in reentrant, up to main trail, north on it to switchback, then cross country, hitting trail to W of no. 4. Dave sees this as big mistake, due to slow running. What about a route...

...curving around N from the switchback through the runnable areas? I think I would have tried that. The fast runner in shape probably does better on G's route, but he noted having to walk up part of the hill to 4 since not in shape.)

4-5: G--Continued up trail, veered off at sharp bend. Made big mistake by looking for control in wrong valley. Careful check of map when found wrong control helped correct quickly. M--Followed path part way, problems on course and a leg that gave many trouble. Marc probably had best split here--doing as Geoff did to bend, then contouring along spur N of stream until reaching control. Dave went around on trail all the way until dropping down to swamp, where he wallowed around for several minutes--Geoff caught him here. Brent Benedek of CVO (Vancouver Is.) was one of two (Geoff the other) to approach control from W. Did he use the small trail at edge of map and then the spur as a hand-rail? Possibly the best rte., avoiding extra climb and distance.)

5-6: G--Ran straight till I stumbled on a trail, then followed trails, planning ahead for no. 7. M--Went straight up hill from 5, followed path, then went up stream. Fell down a couple of times, lost codes and clues. (The small trail G took easy to miss, tho' pacing would prevent error. Clearly the best route. Dave did as M and...

[Photo Caption: Individual champion Geoff Dillard receives his award from Brock Hogle.]

Page 8: Analysis Continued

[Photo: Rick Meeder (r), sport announcer for KOMO-TV evening news, interviews individual overall winners Geoff Dillard (Nathan Hale) and Maia Halvorsen (Collins).]

...then bush whacked. I held my compass at an angle so it didn't work. Made it almost to control, back-tracked, realized my mistake and found control. (One of the better notes it was a poor choice. Marc lost time on this leg, as he doubled back from 5 to trail to S and then cut off before reaching the small trail G took.)

6-7: G--Started to follow stream, then crashed through bushes to it, ran up trail, had trouble once I got to root-stock area, because I thought clues meant NW side of area rather than of one rootstock (G's 1st thought closer to truth--that is, where more than one identical feature within circle, clue will tell which one--in this case the "northwesternmost"). M--Because I lost my clues, I walked right by control, and then it took me a while to find it. (Marc had same problem, but his route otherwise like G's. Dave ran a lot of extra distance, on path and then road to W end of large field, where he went E to trail end.)

7-8: (For all four, straightforward direct route, field to trail to field. Curiously, many others went roundabout on road and then diagonally across last field.)

One final note: Geoff's excellent time, which easily could have been 5 mins. better, was only about 4 mins. behind that of Mike Schuh, who vetted the course (in the process, losing some time though to rehang a control or two). Clearly one key to success is quick recovery from mistakes.

Page 9: Juniors

Organizing an Interscholastic League

Why, I even tried an orienteering exercise with my runners. I made a map of the area around the school, put out surveyor's tape for markers, and told the team to go out and bring back the tapes which they located in going around the course. Problem was, I put magnetic north arrows pointing south... Went out to look for them and found them returning slowly, disgruntled, with armloads of surveyor's tape, which they had diligently picked up at a construction site near the school....

No wonder one group of high schoolers was less than enthusiastic to try orienteering again. Yet here we found a coach who was really interested in the sport, and he was totally unaware that there was such an animal as a local orienteering club that could have helped him. One of the many discoveries made in our initial attempt at forming an interscholastic orienteering league.

Hardly a new idea--interscholastic orienteering--but something we began to work on in the spring of 1982, calling a few of the Seattle area (Metro League) running coaches to see whether there would be any interest ("Tell us more."). In September we sent letters to all of them and a few administrators of smaller, private schools. The letters included a prospectus for a league series, beginning after the conclusion of cross country in mid-November and ending with a championship meet in late February just before the start of track practices. Fee for the 6 meets, $ 10/head; expectation that the school participants would organize themselves and make their own arrangements to get to the meets.

May we come and give a short presentation to your runners and explain our plans? Virtually no response. Ever tried reaching an overworked, underpaid teacher by phone? Difficult, especially when you yourself are an overworked, underpaid teacher. Eventually, presentations made in half a dozen schools, some though after the first meet in the series. In other cases, either the students not interested or the coaches not enough to persuade them to take an interest (anything that seems to suggest extra commitment of time or formal commitment by the school is like a cold shower for enthusiasm). We went to the state cross country championships (should have gone to the city and district ones too), gave out leaflets and even conned the PA announcer into calling attention to us. Some nibbled, one school bit (out of the 2-3000 runners and spectators), most wanted to know whether we were the people giving out programs....

As Bill Shannon of Orienteering Unlimited has pointed out to me (he is a vice-principal in a school that for obvious reasons has a strong orienteering program), you really need an enthusiast within a school to get a start and keep interest going. We did work through our fifth column too, although there we ran into problems with other conflicting programs (private schools tend to have active ski programs, wilderness programs that take the participants away on weekends, etc.). Clearly one thing to do way ahead of time is to check more closely into possible conflicts (e.g., do not schedule a meet the same day as college board exams). The problem seems to be though, finding a schedule to satisfy all is like finding a control in a small pit smack in the middle of a dark green area on the map....

Perhaps the media would help. One of the cross country coaches, who had just been called by the prep reporter for a local paper, passed the message on. How about a story on the league? Well..., we have limited space, and we have to cover the sports that people are interested in, and until it's news.... Hello, this is the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (that's it, make it sound really official and don't let on that it's being run out of your back pocket in this first season). I have some prep results to report. How do you spell that? Oh, what is it, you ask? ...Results in the paper--no attempt to give all the club results, but only the school ones, since school scores do get printed. Hello, you called me about doing a story on or-i-en-teer-ing (boy, that's a mouthful). Well, now that the results have appeared, my editor thinks....I talked with the coaches at...and they referred me to you for more information. Result: a fine story with a photo of the leading school orienteer at the mid-point in the season. Result, further inquiries about the league.

What can we do to keep those who come once, get them to come back for more? After school practice sessions in a local park, and "Chariots of Fire: Newsletter of the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League," containing results after each meet,

Page 10

standings, training tips, route analysis, pictures, features on different schools, Ziggy cartoons, etc. Sure, it is time-consuming and expensive (league revenues do not cover the cost). Each issue is about three 8.5 x 14 sheets, printed with reduced text on both sides--about as much material as goes in many of the larger club newsletters--and appearing within a week following each meet.

The successes are reported elsewhere, but we should ask what we can do to keep the fire burning and build on what we have so far accomplished. One of the messages of Ted Wester, the successful promoter of Australain O, is to be persistent. Be willing to spend a lot of time and some money and not be discouraged. Keep writing the coaches and the media, distribute copies of the newsletter, even if initially you have no response. At least when you approach the next season, they cannot claim never to have heard of the sport. Work harder on planning a schedule that will suit more people's needs. A questionnaire to this year's participants at the end of the season is one step in this direction. In approaching new schools, where possible go to the top--athletic directors may be more inclined to respond and help you set up a presentation. Encourage your orienteers to talk with their friends in other schools. Provide them with brochures that they can give out to the other team at cross country meets in the fall. Try to arrange presentations at coaches' meetings and summer cross country camps. Work up presentations based on your initial successes in which school orienteers will explain (at least on tape, if not in person) why they have found the sport attractive. Try to keep the flame burning by organizing youth teams in the "off season." If you must focus energies, aim at the schools which have successful running programs, since those may have qualities in the coach and students which can most readily be directed toward an interest in orienteering. Where the successful programs take the lead, others are perhaps more likely to follow. Obviously there is no precise correlation here, nor should the focus be only on runners, but the fact that an organization (such as a cross country team) exists should be of real help in providing a nucleus for expansion.
Oh yes, one more thing especially relevant for Seattle--pray for good weather.
--Dan Waugh

P.S.: My special thanks to Nancy Jackson and Mike Schuh for their help this season.

Page 11

First Impressions
Connie Sutthoff

I must admit that I felt somewhat out of place at my first orienteering event last summer. Talk of reentrants, control codes and attack points was completely foreign to me. Nevertheless, I was hooked after that first meet and innocently volunteered at a Nisqually Orienteers meeting to help with registration and timing at a future event.
While perusing the first issue of THE NORTHWEST ORIENTEER, you can imagine the significant rise in blood pressure I experienced at seeing my name listed opposite the foreboding title of "Meet Director". Before allowing complete hysteria to overtake me, I contacted Pat Malloy - cochairperson of NO and an instrumental person in organizing the club activities. She provided me with a "meet kit" containing everything I'd need - forms, compasses, maps, etc. Most importantly, she pointed out a check list from Orienteering for Sport and Pleasure by Bengtsson and Atkinson. It's my firm belief that the world is a safe place when one has one's checklist in hand.
Armed with the necessities, I managed to regain my confidence. Except for some minor pre-meet panicking, I came through the day with little pain and no scars. Would I do it again? You bet - for two reasons.
For me, orienteering events can be rather intimidating with so many competitors wearing Swedish shoes, special sweats, and sporting wierd looking specialty compasses. Worst of all, they have that look. You just know that they never tripped over those deviously placed logs and that the salmonberry parts for them as they go leaping through "vegetation fight". I appreciate seeing mortals like myself working at the meets and hope that other newcomers feel more at ease by my presence at the registration table. Part of my original enchantment for orienteering was due to seeing such a wide variety of people all participating in one sport. It would be a shame if the families carrying babies in backpacks quit coming to meets because they felt out of place.
Secondly, orienteering now heads my list of "Most Enjoyable Activities for a Saturday Afternoon". However, I realize that the only reason I was able to participate in those meets was due to the dedicated souls who designed the courses and registered and timed the participants. Working at a meet helped alleviate at least some of the guilt I felt at having had such a good time while others were stuck with sedentary duties.
I will make some changes the next time I help with an event and I offer these as suggestions to other first time "Meet Directors".

  1. Be prepared to give detailed instructions or provide maps to the event location. I received a long distance call from Oregon and fumbled around (wasting the caller's money in the process) before I finally took his address and promised to send a map.
  2. Be set up on time. It's better to wait around with nothing to do than to have people breathing down your neck because you're five minutes late.
  3. Have someone available just to provide instruction for neophytes. You can't make adequate explanations while registering others and newcomers should not be made to feel they are a bother.
  4. The finish table should be located away from the start table. I realize this is par for the course at the larger Seattle meets. However, I feel it's worthwhile at the smaller meets, also. It takes the pressure off the timer who will not be faced with both starting people off and having to be aware of incoming runners.
  5. Have extra people on hand just in case you need them. I was lucky that familiar faces were around that I could ask for help in calculating and posting times or to check some part of the course that had been questioned.
    Helping with a meet has one benefit I haven't mentioned yet - it gives you an opportunity to meet some of the hardcore competitors who are really very nice people underneath those Swedish sweats.
    And so for those of you who, like myself, still can't remember which color course is the one that's easiest, who still get the contour lines for ridges and reentrants confused, and who invariably find themselves facing 180° in the wrong direction, take heart! We can still offer valuable services to our clubs.

SCHOOLS

Courses; Foiled Again?
Beth Skelton
Naturalist, Rockwoods Reservation, MO

Are you a teacher or youth leader who feels your efforts at course-setting are "foiled" by lack of knowledge or experience? Read on, for some valuable course-setting tips. . .

Orienteering has traditionally been done in a large wooded area which is unfamiliar to the participants. Certain techniques are used to set courses there, which offer the most challenge, with the least opportunity for the participant to get lost.
But what about teachers and youth leaders who want to do orienteering in their own schoolyards or nearby parks? Here the course-setting challenges are much different. The area might be small, very open and well known to the children. Markers can be seen a long distance away and the other participants are visible too.
Crestwood Park is a good example. There is a school at one end. There are some trees but most of it is playing fields and grass. What are some ways to set a good Orienteering course at such a location? (See map on next page.)
One very good way to utilize a small area is to set a "score course." Kany control markers are set out and runners find as many of these as they can in a specified time limit. Since the students themselves pick which markers they will find, and the order in which they will find them, runners go off in all different directions. This results in less chance of participants following each other or a marker being spotted because of the crowd around it.
Try to have the start in the middle of the area. The school on the Crestwood Park map, for instance, would not be very good because the children would all run in the same direction, west. The circular pavillion would be much better, giving more choice of routes.
It is possible to set a standard "point-to-point" course in a small park area. The challenge is to set the course to get the kids to use their maps and compasses, not just see the marker and run to it. Two features at Crestwood Park are very useful for this. First, the slope of the land. It goes down, up, down, up---dividing the park into sections, adding interesting features like ravines and gullies, and limiting visibility. Making even more distinct sections, are the creeks, lined with trees.

Here are some of the thoughts I had when setting this course:
Start to #1: The control marker is just to the other side of the trees, so it isn't visible from the school. With careful map reading and compass work, the student can come across the creek right to it. Otherwise, they might be off a bit and get confused by the other BBQ pits in the area.

#1-#2: Having #2 on the far side of the hill means it cannot be seen until students get over the top. It is a bit trickier having them read the vegetation feature there, than to place the marker on the more obvious backstop or bench.

#2-#3: Route choice! They have to go one way or another around the tennis courts.

#3-#4: Another example of putting the control just to the other side of the trees so it is hidden. But this one is a bit tougher than #1. It takes contour reading to recognize this as a small knoll.

#4-#5: This leg tests precise map reading near the control. There is a lot of playground equipment, BBQ pits and benches to sort out.

#5-#6: This control can be hidden in the ditch.

#6 through #9: When there is nothing else, the school building itself can be used to shield each control location from the last.

Paper plates or margarine tub lids would make good control markers here. They're just big enough that they won't get lost in the grass.

FORUM

USOF Categories of Meets

The last edition of this column was written by Jeff Lewis, OSUOC, on the subject of a regional meet classification system. He pointed out the two most important reasons for a clearly understood and accepted classification system for meets/events. Those reasons are worth repeating: 1) to help orienteers know what to expect at any specific event in terms of map quality, categories of competition, fees, awards, "scope" of the event (regional, local, etc.) and other factors; and 2) to assist orienteers in selecting meets they wish to participate in.
To understand the system of classifying meets into the three categories of A, B, and C, one must look towards the governing bodies of the sport on the national and international level.
The United States Orienteering Federation is the governing body of orienteering in this country and is recognized by the International Orienteering Federation. In turn, USOF recognizes the IOF as the world governing body of orienteering and abides by the rules and regulations of the IOF except when prevented by local conditions, laws and customs. Similarly, orienteering clubs and their members must decide if they recognize the USOF as the governing body of orienteering in the US. Since the clubs in this region are charted by the USOF, it stands to reason that the USOF categories of meets are utilized as a standard of northwest orienteering.
Lets take a look at the meet classification system established by the USOF Technical Committee as defined in the USOF Rules for Orienteering Competition. Although the rules apply only to USOF sanctioned A meets, it does suggest some guidelines for B meets. The three categories of meets defined by the rules are:

  1. 'A' meets are events sanctioned by the USOF Sanctioning Committee and must comply with the provisions outlined in the Rules for Orienteering Competition. [Copies are available from USOF or NWOA for $1.00, which includes postage and handling]
  2. 'B' meets - or regional meets - are those events which may or may not comply with all the provisions of the Rules of Orienteering Competition and are not nationally sanctioned by the USOF Sanctioning Committe. However, the rules do suggest the following guidelines for B meets and are not meant to be binding in any manner:
    a. It is suggested that there be at least a red, white, and either a yellow or orange course (though both are encouraged as well as a blue and green).
    b. It should be an open meet (anyone can compete).
    c. Advertisements and publicity should specify the type of meet, i.e., point-to-point, score, line, etc.
    d. The organizers should provide the competitors with a standard 4 or 5 color orienteering map, an orginal USGS map, or clearly printed black and white map.
    e. The map scale should be somewhere from 1:10,000 to 1:25,000.
    f. Courses should be vetted. (The vetter can be from the hosting club).
    g. There should be sufficient personnel involved in the meet organization to provide effective starting, finishing, and timing procedures. No minimum number will be set because it depends upon the experience and efficiency of the people conducting the meet, but it is recommended that at least four people be involved.
    h. Classes are not specified for B meets.
  3. 'C' meets - or local meets - are not covered in any manner by the Rules for Orienteering Competition.
    Basically, A and B meets are competitive events, where as C meets can vary, and need to be defined by 'type' at the local/regional level.
    The purpose of any meet classification system is not to label one event as superior or inferior to another. Each event must stand on it's own as to whether or not it met the standards for that type of meet. I've participated in several hundred orienteering events across the continent and have enjoyed some excellent competitive orienteering at class A, B, and C meets.
    USOF has set the standards for class A meets, given guidelines for B meets, and left C meets up to the clubs. An improvement to the USOF categories would come in further classifying the C or local events. This is almost a necessity for the very reasons Jeff points out. Towards this endeavor, THE NORTHWEST ORIENTEER will include a column in the next edition of the event schedule to indicated the type of event or meet being organized.
    Clayton Smith

Running the Swedish Five-Day
Jessica Rykken

Never do today what you can put off until tomorrow. Admittedly a cliché, but one that I adhere to religiously. The Swedish O-Ringen took place almost six months ago and it has taken me that many 'tomorrows' to actually put pen to paper. Although perhaps this is not so bad as I can only write about the things that really stick out in my memory.
My journey to Lulea, a small town in northern Sweden not far below the artic circle, was an orienteering exercise in itself. One which entailed taking two planes (complete with delays), several trains and even boats to arrive at my destination three days after my departure from Seattle - this is what is known as 'bargain travelling'. Upon arriving, somewhat disheveled and disoriented (which was to become a habit during the next few days), I was met by Göran Larsson who chauffeured me to the competition center - in his new Saab no less.
Being a typical Northwest orienteer, overwhelmed by the huge numbers at PNWOF-81, I was completely awestruck by the 'O-town' that had sprung up about the competition center. There were complete neighborhoods of trailers and tents, in the outer suburbs, hordes of tents, mostly inhabited by us non-Scandinavian minorities, stretched out into the horizon. Back at the Larssons 'humble' abode (deluxe trailer complete with color TV on which there was coverage of the meet for half an hour each night), I met up with fellow Seattlite Mike Schuh who had just arrived from his travels in Norway and we headed off to stake our claim in the International District.
During the course of the weekend we established a fair size American camp. Being immediately surrouned on all sides by Japanese, Canadians, Brits (who provided unique entertainment with all-night cricket matches) and representatives from roughly twenty five other countries, map exchanging was a constant activity. The 'campsite' was in good order. WC and washing facilities were ingeniously designed, if not keeping with the American tradition of privacy. There was even a temporary supermarket set up for cook-it-yourselfers along with a cafeteria for those not so inclined. The competition center being so vast, a transit system using old Army bikes was organized. The olive drab model put into use was, without a doubt, designed for 'front line' duty along with armored tanks. Entertainment was plentiful with folk and disco dances alike so that, all in all, campsite life was as festive and enjoyable as the actual orienteering events themselves.
I had unsuspectingly enter myself in D17-18A along with 180 others, and Mike in H-21A with his fair share of competition also. Day one arrived, our start times calling us out of our bags at the rather ungodly hour of 5:15, in an effort to catch the 5:45 bus to the meet site. Luckily, as the week wore on, our start times got progressively later. The meet sites were as ingeniously organized as the competition center - the Swedish Army supplying such necessities as pontoon bridges over rivers etc. Facilities were also very good. After staggering over the finish line one could immediately take a shower, which was refreshing if not even close to lukewarm. Those tender individuals who felt the need for a hot shower had to wait to get back to the competition center, where showers were strategically placed behind a barbed wire fence with a three kroner entry fee. With some 20,000 competitors, five separate starting places were needed (on Day 2 the walk to the start alone was over half the distance of my course), as well as five finish chutes - and woe betide anyone who ran up the wrong chute! But the truly amazing sight was the incredible number of people. What with the competitors, their families, officials, etc, etc, it was almost necessary to send up signal flares from the midst of the crowd so that your team mate could find you after finishing - no matter how good an orienteer he/she was.
I will not dwell on my actual experiences in the woods as those details have somewhat faded from my memory - which may be a good thing as I have it on paper somewhere that I did indeed finish last of the qualifiers (putting sone thirty DQ'ers behind me). But aside from being faced with outstanding competition, I have only myself to blame for this less than desirable standing - not being in my best running or orienteering form (my last event having been at Volunteer Park where I lost my pencil three times). Of course, it must also be taken into account that I was perhaps the only competitor without authentic O shoes - even 'gym shorts runner' Mike, had bought himself a complete O wardrobe and this handicap alone, could have added on those extra seconds (or hours).
However, I did not spend all my time huffing and puffing up hills, trying to figure out what those little crosses on the map were supposed to represent, no, I did in fact pick up a few important

Page 16

rules of thumb, which I will now pass on -

  1. With 20,000 competitors on the same map and 180 others running your course, it is amazing how fast you can lose everyone, but this does not necessarily mean you are off track, so don't let it worry you - at least 'till it gets dark (which could, however, leave you out there until winter).
  2. You know you are well off track when you are only in the company of H-50's and D-14's, while running D17-18.
  3. Never try to follow a Swede unless he/she has at least one broken leg - perferably two.
  4. En route to the last control, forget about your map, just head for the loudspeakers.
  5. Thoses little crosses are anthills, and the only one's you'll find are those not marked on the map.
  6. One last word of wisdom - the obvious advantage of a five day meet over a two day meet is that it gives you three more days to comment, after looking at the days results, - "Oh well, there's always tomorrow!"
    I am not trying to imply by all this that I did not enjoy it out there in the wilds, on the contrary, it was some of the finest orienteering I have experienced. Organization was excellent, courses were challenging and terrain was ideal. Being accustomed to the jungle of devil's club and nettles of Seattle's parks, the open runnable forests were a treat. After getting over my initial frustrations and facing that I could not expect to compete with the Scandinavians, I really enjoyed myself and the scenery.
    Well, the five-days over, my T-shirt and postcards bought and Mike and his tent heading back to Norway, I left Lulea after only one week. I don't think any of us Americans exactly triumphed in our respective classes but I'm sure I speak for everyone when I say that the O-Ringen was an orienteering experience extraordinairre at least for those of us new to grand scale European O. I feel lucky that as a Northwest orienteer I have had the chance to see orienteering at such a level of participation, organization and overall BIGNESS. But just wait, with the pace at which Northwest orienteering is catching on, in a few years.....

REGIONAL NEWS

2-Day Spring Meet
The OSU Orienteering Club will be hosting its Third Annual Spring Meet on May 7-8, 1983. A new site will be used in place of Cow Creek: the vicinity of Arnold Ice Cave (less than 30-minutes south of Bend in the Deschutes National Forest) which will offer participants numerous options for food and shelter.
The area consists of mile after mile of rolling desert covered with sagebrush, juniper, and pine trees. Vegetation is quite variable; the site is generally not as open as the Cow Creek Area. However, the vegetation is sufficiently open to yield very accurate topography with a 10-ft contour interval. Hundreds of small hills, depressions, and reentrants dot the terrain, most of which is at 4400-5100 ft elevation. Man-made features consist of fences and jeep trails. Rock features are numerous -- from boulders to rocky knolls to large 15 meter cliffs -- but are absent in most of the sandy terrain. (For a preview of the topography, consult the USGS quad "Kelsey Butte".)
A new, black-and-white, 1:15000 map will be used. Methods to vastly improve the quality of b/w maps have recently been developed within OSUOC. The maps to be used at this meet will apply these methods and may be expected to be detailed and highly legible.
Five or six courses will be offered, ranging from 2-12 km in length, Awards will be given to individuals as well as a trophy to the top organization. Scoring for the organizational trophy will be based on a point system utilizing the results of all club members... not just the elite men and women. Additionally, teams of 3-5 members may compete on the same course and score points toward the championship trophy.
Preregistration is encouraged so that we may effectively plan the meet. However, registration fees ($5.00 per person or $3.00 per day) need not be paid until the day of the meet. Please send a letter with names and intended courses to OSUOC, as early as possible. (Address is inside newsletter cover.)

Eastern Slope O'
The unique landscape of Central Washington presents special problems to the orienteer. Many of us have run on the Cle Elum Ridge map. The ridge is on the eastern edge of the Pacific Coast/Cascade forested area. Farther east, things change. The land has one or both of two dominant characteristics: flat and dry. Most of the local maps used by EOC do not have contour lines. A contour interval of 100 cm or ½ m is not going to be obvious enough to aid in orienteering and would be difficult to map in the first place. Ther is ONE hill in Ellensburg, which fortunately has a park on it. The map has a contour interval of 1 m.
As the climate in the Kittitas Valley is dry, with about 25 cm of annual precipitation, our local maps do not have "forested areas." Single trees or even shrubs are rare enough. The new Durr Road map, covering about 2 square miles, has not a single tree.

Setting courses on these maps presents problems, especially for the more difficult. Imaginative techniques are used to compensate. Small markers and well-hidden placements are used. A marker for an easier course can be used as a decoy if placed in visible location near the harder-course marker. Vague control features can be used. For instance, a marker location will be shown precisely on the base map, set low enough to not be easily visible, and a control description of "bottom of the hill" will be used. Techniques of this type encourage compass work, of course. Distance can be used to substitute for difficulty. Many of these techniques are familiar to anyone who has orienteered or set courses on small parks and similar areas. The local maps around Ellensburg are generally useful for perfecting techniques, which we can then use on some of the true wilderness maps of Seattle, such as St. Edwards and Discovery Park.

THE NORTHWEST ORIENTEER The Journal of the Northwest Orienteering Association
[Photo: A black and white photograph of a young orienteer in a dark jacket running through a wooded area, holding a map. Another person is visible in the background standing on the trail.]

Front Cover: Kevin Winston (Collins) sloshing through the mud on his way to a first place finish for junior high men; Linda Mayhak of Yelm in bckgd.
COC: Carol Johnson, 4916 Whitman Ave. N, #105, Seattle, WA 98103
EOC: Dale Width, 516 W 13th, Ellensburg, WA 98926
NO: Clayton Smith, 4338 East F St, Tacoma, WA 98404
OSUOC: MU East, OSU, Corvallis, OR 97331
SOC: Dave Enger, PO Box 3682, Bellevue, WA 98009
Publisher: NORTHWEST ORIENTEERING ASSOCIATION, PO Box 1582, Tacoma, WA 98401

There were trophies and cheers for winners, but the fact is everyone won at the first Washington Interscholastic Orienteering Championship held at St. Edward State Park, February 26. The real victor in the season capped by the meet was the sport of orienteering in the Northwest.

The day's glories were shared by teams and individuals from nine different schools and a total of 50 participants on the school course of 2.7 km. (8 controls), designed by Jean Davis. Nathan Hale High School, defeated only once during the regular season, was the runaway team winner, led by Geoff Dillard's outstanding 35:46, some 7 mins. ahead of the next finisher, Brent Benedek of the Cowichan Valley Orienteers (B.C.) and nearly 12 mins. ahead of Marc Olson of Yelm high School. Marc's run was his best of the season, but he had many anxious moments until the control card he dropped after no. 2 was found, preventing disqualification.

Marc's fine run paced Yelm to its best finish of the season, a solid second, and the surprising Blanchet team, a latecomer to the league, was third, paced by John Macaulay's strong 6th place--not bad for a first-time orienteer! Probably the most disappointed team was Lakeside, which had compiled the second best regular-season record. Some of its best orienteers were on a school hike; others were plagued by misfortune--Joe Curren DQ'd, and Woody Rice lost a huge amount of time looking for a control that had disappeared by the time he reached the location.

The consolation for Lakeside was in having the top two high school women, Mara Charnell in first (she was also the season leader) and Kirsten Halvorsen. Third among the H.S. women was Gitte Larsen of Nathan Hale, who surprised her teammate David Ovens (one of the men's leaders all season) and provided valuable points for Hale's team total.
But the star among the women was Maia Halvorsen, the only junior high runner among the women, and the overall woman's winner by nearly two minutes.
What is it? The Prep Page

...Waugh, a professor at the University of Washington who is vice president of the Cascade Orienteering Club, says: "This is a major international sport, so it's worth trying to promote. I'd like to see some Americans get to the international level. When Americans go over there (to Europe) they don't do anything."
...Hale's Geoff Dillard, who finished first and second in the first two prep meets, explained the competition.
In Dillard's second orienteering race, "I got messed up really bad on the second control and I got beat by one of my teammates, Dave Ovens, by about seven seconds."

"There is no way to cheat the system by following another runner," Dillard explained. "You have staggered starts about 4 minutes apart. If you run across someone, you don't trust someone else's way any more than you trust your own."...
[Photo Caption: Hale's Geoff Dillard, map in hand, runs past a marker, right, on an orienteering course]
...Her Collins Academy teammate, Kevin Winston, who at times during the season was his school's lone participant, received a well-deserved trophy for the junior high men.

We had all been anticipating with great interest the participation of two Canadian school teams. And we were not disappointed. The Cowichan Valley Orienteers from Vancouver Island had the overall second and fifth place finishers, which gave them a solid edge over the OUBC Junior "Deltoids," but not enough to beat Nathan Hale for the international challenge trophy that was awarded.

Nathan Hale received yet another, unexpected award, a trophy donated by Mr. and Mrs. Brock Hogle and named (somewhat embarrassingly) for the undersigned. This impressive cup will be engraved with the winning team's name and will serve as a perpetual trophy for the league. Mr. Hogle, the long-time and extremely successful cross country coach at Nathan Hale H.S., was an early supporter of the league as we began organizing last year, and he kindly agreed to present the awards for this championship. We are most grateful to him for his support.

Not the least of the pleasant surprises for the day was the appearance of a camera crew from KOMO-TV and the KOMO evening news sports commentator, Rick Meeder. They put together a fine little segment on the 6:30 news, which succeeded in giving a good idea of the sport in a concise and very interesting way.

Many thanks are due to others--Mike Schuh and Debbie Newell for having the revised map ready in time, St. Thomas Center for the map sponsorship (and Dave Enger for obtaining the sponsorship), Debbie and Arnold Kay for making the lovely trophies, Jean for her courses, the Millers for registration, Einar Storakers and Harvey Friedman for finish timing, Harry Morgan and Barbara Pedersen for starts, and Nancy Lewis for organizing and contributing to the feeding of hungry orienteers. Many others assisted, whose services if not names, are hereby inscribed for the centuries....
Strategy on difficult courses involves things like, do you go up the hill or go around? "The more tired you get, the more difficult it is to make this decision," Waugh said.
Despite Waugh's enthusiasm for the sport, he has had trouble "selling it."
Brock Hogle, Hale cross country coach, said, "About five or six of our kids do it. It's got a lot of promise. Most of the cross-country kids are pretty smart. You get to use your brains."
What are the chances of the sport's acceptance by the high schools?

Lakeside coach Hugh Tower said, "There are maybe a half dozen of my cross-country kids running. The guy who is handling it is Peter Hayes in our wilderness department. There are quite a few other kids who are not involved in cross country but they do wilderness. They seem to be having an awful lot of fun."

"C.J. Blagg, who's probably my top boy, has been active in it. I think it's fine. It gives them something different to do. I encourage them to keep active. Tom Mills plays basketball. Some are running road runs and marathons. Whatever they do is fine."
[Photo Caption: The Nathan Hale Championship team (l to r): Geoff Dillard, Mike McCool, David Ovens, Dave Steele, and Gitte Larsen, with cross country coach Brock Hogle.]

Note that the team results were calculated on the basis of four placings, with placings being given to a maximum of seven members on a team. For the Washington schools, Canadian placings were not included, nor were those of participants who were not part of a full team.

0-1: G--Read map while running towards trail, so by the time I had to concentrate on the steep terrain I knew where I was going. Looked ahead on map for no. 2. (G and third-place finisher Dave Steele stayed in open area at top of hill as long as possible, instead of taking first trail down.)
[Photo Caption: Maia Halvorsen of Collins, overall women's champ, receives her award from Brock Hogle.]
St. Edward State Park / St. Thomas Center
[Image: Detailed orienteering map of St. Edward State Park showing route lines of four runners.]

...curving around N from the switchback through the runnable areas? I think I would have tried that. The fast runner in shape probably does better on G's route, but he noted having to walk up part of the hill to 4 since not in shape.)

4-5: G--Continued up trail, veered off at sharp bend. Made big mistake by looking for control in wrong valley. Careful check of map when found wrong control helped correct quickly. M--Followed path part way, problems on course and a leg that gave many trouble. Marc probably had best split here--doing as Geoff did to bend, then contouring along spur N of stream until reaching control. Dave went around on trail all the way until dropping down to swamp, where he wallowed around for several minutes--Geoff caught him here. Brent Benedek of CVO (Vancouver Is.) was one of two (Geoff the other) to approach control from W. Did he use the small trail at edge of map and then the spur as a hand-rail? Possibly the best rte., avoiding extra climb and distance.)

5-6: G--Ran straight till I stumbled on a trail, then followed trails, planning ahead for no. 7. M--Went straight up hill from 5, followed path, then went up stream. Fell down a couple of times, lost codes and clues. (The small trail G took easy to miss, tho' pacing would prevent error. Clearly the best route. Dave did as M and...
[Photo Caption: Individual champion Geoff Dillard receives his award from Brock Hogle.]
[Photo: Rick Meeder (r), sport announcer for KOMO-TV evening news, interviews individual overall winners Geoff Dillard (Nathan Hale) and Maia Halvorsen (Collins).]

...then bush whacked. I held my compass at an angle so it didn't work. Made it almost to control, back-tracked, realized my mistake and found control. (One of the better notes it was a poor choice. Marc lost time on this leg, as he doubled back from 5 to trail to S and then cut off before reaching the small trail G took.)

6-7: G--Started to follow stream, then crashed through bushes to it, ran up trail, had trouble once I got to root-stock area, because I thought clues meant NW side of area rather than of one rootstock (G's 1st thought closer to truth--that is, where more than one identical feature within circle, clue will tell which one--in this case the "northwesternmost"). M--Because I lost my clues, I walked right by control, and then it took me a while to find it. (Marc had same problem, but his route otherwise like G's. Dave ran a lot of extra distance, on path and then road to W end of large field, where he went E to trail end.)
7-8: (For all four, straightforward direct route, field to trail to field. Curiously, many others went roundabout on road and then diagonally across last field.)

One final note: Geoff's excellent time, which easily could have been 5 mins. better, was only about 4 mins. behind that of Mike Schuh, who vetted the course (in the process, losing some time though to rehang a control or two). Clearly one key to success is quick recovery from mistakes.
[Photo Caption: Coach Brock Hogle of Nathan Hale presents H. S. Women's championship plaque to Mara Charnell of Lakeside.]

Why, I even tried an orienteering exercise with my runners. I made a map of the area around the school, put out surveyor's tape for markers, and told the team to go out and bring back the tapes which they located in going around the course. Problem was, I put magnetic north arrows pointing south... Went out to look for them and found them returning slowly, disgruntled, with armloads of surveyor's tape, which they had diligently picked up at a construction site near the school....

No wonder one group of high schoolers was less than enthusiastic to try orienteering again. Yet here we found a coach who was really interested in the sport, and he was totally unaware that there was such an animal as a local orienteering club that could have helped him. One of the many discoveries made in our initial attempt at forming an interscholastic orienteering league.

Hardly a new idea--interscholastic orienteering--but something we began to work on in the spring of 1982, calling a few of the Seattle area (Metro League) running coaches to see whether there would be any interest ("Tell us more."). In September we sent letters to all of them and a few administrators of smaller, private schools. The letters included a prospectus for a league series, beginning after the conclusion of cross country in mid-November and ending with a championship meet in late February just before the start of track practices. Fee for the 6 meets, $ 10/head; expectation that the school participants would organize themselves and make their own arrangements to get to the meets.

May we come and give a short presentation to your runners and explain our plans? Virtually no response. Ever tried reaching an overworked, underpaid teacher by phone? Difficult, especially when you yourself are an overworked, underpaid teacher. Eventually, presentations made in half a dozen schools, some though after the first meet in the series. In other cases, either the students not interested or the coaches not enough to persuade them to take an interest (anything that seems to suggest extra commitment of time or formal commitment by the school is like a cold shower for enthusiasm). We went to the state cross country championships (should have gone to the city and district ones too), gave out leaflets and even conned the PA announcer into calling attention to us. Some nibbled, one school bit (out of the 2-3000 runners and spectators), most wanted to know whether we were the people giving out programs....

As Bill Shannon of Orienteering Unlimited has pointed out to me (he is a vice-principal in a school that for obvious reasons has a strong orienteering program), you really need an enthusiast within a school to get a start and keep interest going. We did work through our fifth column too, although there we ran into problems with other conflicting programs (private schools tend to have active ski programs, wilderness programs that take the participants away on weekends, etc.). Clearly one thing to do way ahead of time is to check more closely into possible conflicts (e.g., do not schedule a meet the same day as college board exams). The problem seems to be though, finding a schedule to satisfy all is like finding a control in a small pit smack in the middle of a dark green area on the map....

Perhaps the media would help. One of the cross country coaches, who had just been called by the prep reporter for a local paper, passed the message on. How about a story on the league? Well..., we have limited space, and we have to cover the sports that people are interested in, and until it's news.... Hello, this is the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League (that's it, make it sound really official and don't let on that it's being run out of your back pocket in this first season). I have some prep results to report. How do you spell that? Oh, what is it, you ask? ...Results in the paper--no attempt to give all the club results, but only the school ones, since school scores do get printed. Hello, you called me about doing a story on or-i-en-teer-ing (boy, that's a mouthful). Well, now that the results have appeared, my editor thinks....I talked with the coaches at...and they referred me to you for more information. Result: a fine story with a photo of the leading school orienteer at the mid-point in the season. Result, further inquiries about the league.

What can we do to keep those who come once, get them to come back for more? After school practice sessions in a local park, and "Chariots of Fire: Newsletter of the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League," containing results after each meet,

standings, training tips, route analysis, pictures, features on different schools, Ziggy cartoons, etc. Sure, it is time-consuming and expensive (league revenues do not cover the cost). Each issue is about three 8.5 x 14 sheets, printed with reduced text on both sides--about as much material as goes in many of the larger club newsletters--and appearing within a week following each meet.

The successes are reported elsewhere, but we should ask what we can do to keep the fire burning and build on what we have so far accomplished. One of the messages of Ted Wester, the successful promoter of Australain O, is to be persistent. Be willing to spend a lot of time and some money and not be discouraged. Keep writing the coaches and the media, distribute copies of the newsletter, even if initially you have no response. At least when you approach the next season, they cannot claim never to have heard of the sport. Work harder on planning a schedule that will suit more people's needs. A questionnaire to this year's participants at the end of the season is one step in this direction. In approaching new schools, where possible go to the top--athletic directors may be more inclined to respond and help you set up a presentation. Encourage your orienteers to talk with their friends in other schools. Provide them with brochures that they can give out to the other team at cross country meets in the fall. Try to arrange presentations at coaches' meetings and summer cross country camps. Work up presentations based on your initial successes in which school orienteers will explain (at least on tape, if not in person) why they have found the sport attractive. Try to keep the flame burning by organizing youth teams in the "off season." If you must focus energies, aim at the schools which have successful running programs, since those may have qualities in the coach and students which can most readily be directed toward an interest in orienteering. Where the successful programs take the lead, others are perhaps more likely to follow. Obviously there is no precise correlation here, nor should the focus be only on runners, but the fact that an organization (such as a cross country team) exists should be of real help in providing a nucleus for expansion. Oh yes, one more thing especially relevant for Seattle--pray for good weather. --Dan Waugh
P.S.: My special thanks to Nancy Jackson and Mike Schuh for their help this season.

I must admit that I felt somewhat out of place at my first orienteering event last summer. Talk of reentrants, control codes and attack points was completely foreign to me. Nevertheless, I was hooked after that first meet and innocently volunteered at a Nisqually Orienteers meeting to help with registration and timing at a future event. While perusing the first issue of THE NORTHWEST ORIENTEER, you can imagine the significant rise in blood pressure I experienced at seeing my name listed opposite the foreboding title of "Meet Director". Before allowing complete hysteria to overtake me, I contacted Pat Malloy - cochairperson of NO and an instrumental person in organizing the club activities. She provided me with a "meet kit" containing everything I'd need - forms, compasses, maps, etc. Most importantly, she pointed out a check list from Orienteering for Sport and Pleasure by Bengtsson and Atkinson. It's my firm belief that the world is a safe place when one has one's checklist in hand. Armed with the necessities, I managed to regain my confidence. Except for some minor pre-meet panicking, I came through the day with little pain and no scars. Would I do it again? You bet - for two reasons. For me, orienteering events can be rather intimidating with so many competitors wearing Swedish shoes, special sweats, and sporting wierd looking specialty compasses. Worst of all, they have that look. You just know that they never tripped over those deviously placed logs and that the salmonberry parts for them as they go leaping through "vegetation fight". I appreciate seeing mortals like myself working at the meets and hope that other newcomers feel more at ease by my presence at the registration table. Part of my original enchantment for orienteering was due to seeing such a wide variety of people all participating in one sport. It would be a shame if the families carrying babies in backpacks quit coming to meets because they felt out of place. Secondly, orienteering now heads my list of "Most Enjoyable Activities for a Saturday Afternoon". However, I realize that the only reason I was able to participate in those meets was due to the dedicated souls who designed the courses and registered and timed the participants. Working at a meet helped alleviate at least some of the guilt I felt at having had such a good time while others were stuck with sedentary duties. I will make some changes the next time I help with an event and I offer these as suggestions to other first time "Meet Directors".

Be prepared to give detailed instructions or provide maps to the event location. I received a long distance call from Oregon and fumbled around (wasting the caller's money in the process) before I finally took his address and promised to send a map.
Be set up on time. It's better to wait around with nothing to do than to have people breathing down your neck because you're five minutes late.
Have someone available just to provide instruction for neophytes. You can't make adequate explanations while registering others and newcomers should not be made to feel they are a bother.

The finish table should be located away from the start table. I realize this is par for the course at the larger Seattle meets. However, I feel it's worthwhile at the smaller meets, also. It takes the pressure off the timer who will not be faced with both starting people off and having to be aware of incoming runners.

Have extra people on hand just in case you need them. I was lucky that familiar faces were around that I could ask for help in calculating and posting times or to check some part of the course that had been questioned. Helping with a meet has one benefit I haven't mentioned yet - it gives you an opportunity to meet some of the hardcore competitors who are really very nice people underneath those Swedish sweats. And so for those of you who, like myself, still can't remember which color course is the one that's easiest, who still get the contour lines for ridges and reentrants confused, and who invariably find themselves facing 180° in the wrong direction, take heart! We can still offer valuable services to our clubs.

Courses; Foiled Again? Beth Skelton Naturalist, Rockwoods Reservation, MO
Are you a teacher or youth leader who feels your efforts at course-setting are "foiled" by lack of knowledge or experience? Read on, for some valuable course-setting tips. . .

Orienteering has traditionally been done in a large wooded area which is unfamiliar to the participants. Certain techniques are used to set courses there, which offer the most challenge, with the least opportunity for the participant to get lost. But what about teachers and youth leaders who want to do orienteering in their own schoolyards or nearby parks? Here the course-setting challenges are much different. The area might be small, very open and well known to the children. Markers can be seen a long distance away and the other participants are visible too. Crestwood Park is a good example. There is a school at one end. There are some trees but most of it is playing fields and grass. What are some ways to set a good Orienteering course at such a location? (See map on next page.) One very good way to utilize a small area is to set a "score course." Kany control markers are set out and runners find as many of these as they can in a specified time limit. Since the students themselves pick which markers they will find, and the order in which they will find them, runners go off in all different directions. This results in less chance of participants following each other or a marker being spotted because of the crowd around it. Try to have the start in the middle of the area. The school on the Crestwood Park map, for instance, would not be very good because the children would all run in the same direction, west. The circular pavillion would be much better, giving more choice of routes. It is possible to set a standard "point-to-point" course in a small park area. The challenge is to set the course to get the kids to use their maps and compasses, not just see the marker and run to it. Two features at Crestwood Park are very useful for this. First, the slope of the land. It goes down, up, down, up---dividing the park into sections, adding interesting features like ravines and gullies, and limiting visibility. Making even more distinct sections, are the creeks, lined with trees.

Here are some of the thoughts I had when setting this course: Start to #1: The control marker is just to the other side of the trees, so it isn't visible from the school. With careful map reading and compass work, the student can come across the creek right to it. Otherwise, they might be off a bit and get confused by the other BBQ pits in the area.

#1-#2: Having #2 on the far side of the hill means it cannot be seen until students get over the top. It is a bit trickier having them read the vegetation feature there, than to place the marker on the more obvious backstop or bench.
#2-#3: Route choice! They have to go one way or another around the tennis courts.
#3-#4: Another example of putting the control just to the other side of the trees so it is hidden. But this one is a bit tougher than #1. It takes contour reading to recognize this as a small knoll.
[Map of Crestwood Park Orienteering Map] Reprinted from the ORA Journal, Winter 1979
#4-#5: This leg tests precise map reading near the control. There is a lot of playground equipment, BBQ pits and benches to sort out.
#5-#6: This control can be hidden in the ditch.
#6 through #9: When there is nothing else, the school building itself can be used to shield each control location from the last.
Paper plates or margarine tub lids would make good control markers here. They're just big enough that they won't get lost in the grass.

The last edition of this column was written by Jeff Lewis, OSUOC, on the subject of a regional meet classification system. He pointed out the two most important reasons for a clearly understood and accepted classification system for meets/events. Those reasons are worth repeating: 1) to help orienteers know what to expect at any specific event in terms of map quality, categories of competition, fees, awards, "scope" of the event (regional, local, etc.) and other factors; and 2) to assist orienteers in selecting meets they wish to participate in. To understand the system of classifying meets into the three categories of A, B, and C, one must look towards the governing bodies of the sport on the national and international level. The United States Orienteering Federation is the governing body of orienteering in this country and is recognized by the International Orienteering Federation. In turn, USOF recognizes the IOF as the world governing body of orienteering and abides by the rules and regulations of the IOF except when prevented by local conditions, laws and customs. Similarly, orienteering clubs and their members must decide if they recognize the USOF as the governing body of orienteering in the US. Since the clubs in this region are charted by the USOF, it stands to reason that the USOF categories of meets are utilized as a standard of northwest orienteering. Lets take a look at the meet classification system established by the USOF Technical Committee as defined in the USOF Rules for Orienteering Competition. Although the rules apply only to USOF sanctioned A meets, it does suggest some guidelines for B meets. The three categories of meets defined by the rules are:

'A' meets are events sanctioned by the USOF Sanctioning Committee and must comply with the provisions outlined in the Rules for Orienteering Competition. [Copies are available from USOF or NWOA for $1.00, which includes postage and handling]

'B' meets - or regional meets - are those events which may or may not comply with all the provisions of the Rules of Orienteering Competition and are not nationally sanctioned by the USOF Sanctioning Committe. However, the rules do suggest the following guidelines for B meets and are not meant to be binding in any manner: a. It is suggested that there be at least a red, white, and either a yellow or orange course (though both are encouraged as well as a blue and green). b. It should be an open meet (anyone can compete). c. Advertisements and publicity should specify the type of meet, i.e., point-to-point, score, line, etc. d. The organizers should provide the competitors with a standard 4 or 5 color orienteering map, an orginal USGS map, or clearly printed black and white map. e. The map scale should be somewhere from 1:10,000 to 1:25,000. f. Courses should be vetted. (The vetter can be from the hosting club). g. There should be sufficient personnel involved in the meet organization to provide effective starting, finishing, and timing procedures. No minimum number will be set because it depends upon the experience and efficiency of the people conducting the meet, but it is recommended that at least four people be involved. h. Classes are not specified for B meets.

'C' meets - or local meets - are not covered in any manner by the Rules for Orienteering Competition. Basically, A and B meets are competitive events, where as C meets can vary, and need to be defined by 'type' at the local/regional level. The purpose of any meet classification system is not to label one event as superior or inferior to another. Each event must stand on it's own as to whether or not it met the standards for that type of meet. I've participated in several hundred orienteering events across the continent and have enjoyed some excellent competitive orienteering at class A, B, and C meets. USOF has set the standards for class A meets, given guidelines for B meets, and left C meets up to the clubs. An improvement to the USOF categories would come in further classifying the C or local events. This is almost a necessity for the very reasons Jeff points out. Towards this endeavor, THE NORTHWEST ORIENTEER will include a column in the next edition of the event schedule to indicated the type of event or meet being organized. Clayton Smith
Running the Swedish Five-Day Jessica Rykken

Never do today what you can put off until tomorrow. Admittedly a cliché, but one that I adhere to religiously. The Swedish O-Ringen took place almost six months ago and it has taken me that many 'tomorrows' to actually put pen to paper. Although perhaps this is not so bad as I can only write about the things that really stick out in my memory. My journey to Lulea, a small town in northern Sweden not far below the artic circle, was an orienteering exercise in itself. One which entailed taking two planes (complete with delays), several trains and even boats to arrive at my destination three days after my departure from Seattle - this is what is known as 'bargain travelling'. Upon arriving, somewhat disheveled and disoriented (which was to become a habit during the next few days), I was met by Göran Larsson who chauffeured me to the competition center - in his new Saab no less. Being a typical Northwest orienteer, overwhelmed by the huge numbers at PNWOF-81, I was completely awestruck by the 'O-town' that had sprung up about the competition center. There were complete neighborhoods of trailers and tents, in the outer suburbs, hordes of tents, mostly inhabited by us non-Scandinavian minorities, stretched out into the horizon. Back at the Larssons 'humble' abode (deluxe trailer complete with color TV on which there was coverage of the meet for half an hour each night), I met up with fellow Seattlite Mike Schuh who had just arrived from his travels in Norway and we headed off to stake our claim in the International District. During the course of the weekend we established a fair size American camp. Being immediately surrouned on all sides by Japanese, Canadians, Brits (who provided unique entertainment with all-night cricket matches) and representatives from roughly twenty five other countries, map exchanging was a constant activity. The 'campsite' was in good order. WC and washing facilities were ingeniously designed, if not keeping with the American tradition of privacy. There was even a temporary supermarket set up for cook-it-yourselfers along with a cafeteria for those not so inclined. The competition center being so vast, a transit system using old Army bikes was organized. The olive drab model put into use was, without a doubt, designed for 'front line' duty along with armored tanks. Entertainment was plentiful with folk and disco dances alike so that, all in all, campsite life was as festive and enjoyable as the actual orienteering events themselves. I had unsuspectingly enter myself in D17-18A along with 180 others, and Mike in H-21A with his fair share of competition also. Day one arrived, our start times calling us out of our bags at the rather ungodly hour of 5:15, in an effort to catch the 5:45 bus to the meet site. Luckily, as the week wore on, our start times got progressively later. The meet sites were as ingeniously organized as the competition center - the Swedish Army supplying such necessities as pontoon bridges over rivers etc. Facilities were also very good. After staggering over the finish line one could immediately take a shower, which was refreshing if not even close to lukewarm. Those tender individuals who felt the need for a hot shower had to wait to get back to the competition center, where showers were strategically placed behind a barbed wire fence with a three kroner entry fee. With some 20,000 competitors, five separate starting places were needed (on Day 2 the walk to the start alone was over half the distance of my course), as well as five finish chutes - and woe betide anyone who ran up the wrong chute! But the truly amazing sight was the incredible number of people. What with the competitors, their families, officials, etc, etc, it was almost necessary to send up signal flares from the midst of the crowd so that your team mate could find you after finishing - no matter how good an orienteer he/she was. I will not dwell on my actual experiences in the woods as those details have somewhat faded from my memory - which may be a good thing as I have it on paper somewhere that I did indeed finish last of the qualifiers (putting sone thirty DQ'ers behind me). But aside from being faced with outstanding competition, I have only myself to blame for this less than desirable standing - not being in my best running or orienteering form (my last event having been at Volunteer Park where I lost my pencil three times). Of course, it must also be taken into account that I was perhaps the only competitor without authentic O shoes - even 'gym shorts runner' Mike, had bought himself a complete O wardrobe and this handicap alone, could have added on those extra seconds (or hours). However, I did not spend all my time huffing and puffing up hills, trying to figure out what those little crosses on the map were supposed to represent, no, I did in fact pick up a few important
rules of thumb, which I will now pass on -

With 20,000 competitors on the same map and 180 others running your course, it is amazing how fast you can lose everyone, but this does not necessarily mean you are off track, so don't let it worry you - at least 'till it gets dark (which could, however, leave you out there until winter).
You know you are well off track when you are only in the company of H-50's and D-14's, while running D17-18.
Never try to follow a Swede unless he/she has at least one broken leg - perferably two.
En route to the last control, forget about your map, just head for the loudspeakers.
Thoses little crosses are anthills, and the only one's you'll find are those not marked on the map.

One last word of wisdom - the obvious advantage of a five day meet over a two day meet is that it gives you three more days to comment, after looking at the days results, - "Oh well, there's always tomorrow!" I am not trying to imply by all this that I did not enjoy it out there in the wilds, on the contrary, it was some of the finest orienteering I have experienced. Organization was excellent, courses were challenging and terrain was ideal. Being accustomed to the jungle of devil's club and nettles of Seattle's parks, the open runnable forests were a treat. After getting over my initial frustrations and facing that I could not expect to compete with the Scandinavians, I really enjoyed myself and the scenery. Well, the five-days over, my T-shirt and postcards bought and Mike and his tent heading back to Norway, I left Lulea after only one week. I don't think any of us Americans exactly triumphed in our respective classes but I'm sure I speak for everyone when I say that the O-Ringen was an orienteering experience extraordinairre at least for those of us new to grand scale European O. I feel lucky that as a Northwest orienteer I have had the chance to see orienteering at such a level of participation, organization and overall BIGNESS. But just wait, with the pace at which Northwest orienteering is catching on, in a few years.....
