2-Day Spring Meet The OSU Orienteering Club will be hosting its Third Annual Spring Meet on May 7-8, 1983. A new site will be used in place of Cow Creek: the vicinity of Arnold Ice Cave (less than 30-minutes south of Bend in the Deschutes National Forest) which will offer participants numerous options for food and shelter. The area consists of mile after mile of rolling desert covered with sagebrush, juniper, and pine trees. Vegetation is quite variable; the site is generally not as open as the Cow Creek Area. However, the vegetation is sufficiently open to yield very accurate topography with a 10-ft contour interval. Hundreds of small hills, depressions, and reentrants dot the terrain, most of which is at 4400-5100 ft elevation. Man-made features consist of fences and jeep trails. Rock features are numerous -- from boulders to rocky knolls to large 15 meter cliffs -- but are absent in most of the sandy terrain. (For a preview of the topography, consult the USGS quad "Kelsey Butte".) A new, black-and-white, 1:15000 map will be used. Methods to vastly improve the quality of b/w maps have recently been developed within OSUOC. The maps to be used at this meet will apply these methods and may be expected to be detailed and highly legible. Five or six courses will be offered, ranging from 2-12 km in length, Awards will be given to individuals as well as a trophy to the top organization. Scoring for the organizational trophy will be based on a point system utilizing the results of all club members... not just the elite men and women. Additionally, teams of 3-5 members may compete on the same course and score points toward the championship trophy. Preregistration is encouraged so that we may effectively plan the meet. However, registration fees ($5.00 per person or $3.00 per day) need not be paid until the day of the meet. Please send a letter with names and intended courses to OSUOC, as early as possible. (Address is inside newsletter cover.)

Eastern Slope O' The unique landscape of Central Washington presents special problems to the orienteer. Many of us have run on the Cle Elum Ridge map. The ridge is on the eastern edge of the Pacific Coast/Cascade forested area. Farther east, things change. The land has one or both of two dominant characteristics: flat and dry. Most of the local maps used by EOC do not have contour lines. A contour interval of 100 cm or ½ m is not going to be obvious enough to aid in orienteering and would be difficult to map in the first place. Ther is ONE hill in Ellensburg, which fortunately has a park on it. The map has a contour interval of 1 m. As the climate in the Kittitas Valley is dry, with about 25 cm of annual precipitation, our local maps do not have "forested areas." Single trees or even shrubs are rare enough. The new Durr Road map, covering about 2 square miles, has not a single tree.

Setting courses on these maps presents problems, especially for the more difficult. Imaginative techniques are used to compensate. Small markers and well-hidden placements are used. A marker for an easier course can be used as a decoy if placed in visible location near the harder-course marker. Vague control features can be used. For instance, a marker location will be shown precisely on the base map, set low enough to not be easily visible, and a control description of "bottom of the hill" will be used. Techniques of this type encourage compass work, of course. Distance can be used to substitute for difficulty. Many of these techniques are familiar to anyone who has orienteered or set courses on small parks and similar areas. The local maps around Ellensburg are generally useful for perfecting techniques, which we can then use on some of the true wilderness maps of Seattle, such as St. Edwards and Discovery Park.

I just finished reading the premier issue of The Northwest Orienteer. I must say that I found it very interesting. Especially enjoyable were the articles on British and Australian orienteering. My husband and I are planning a trip to Australia and were surprised to learn that we will be able to partake in orienteering while on vacation. We're looking forward to meeting orienteers from "down under" and your next issue. Kathleen Fleser Ft Lewis Youth Activities Director

At the January 18th Executive Board meeting of COC, it was unanimously voted that COC commit to The Northwest Orienteer for the entire 1983 year with the proviso that things improve noticeably. Some areas for improvement are: 1) spelling, grammer and general proofreading; 2) timeliness; and 3) content. Since it is intended as a regional magazine, we feel that photos and articles should be about orienteering in the region, not internationally. I feel that one page per issue is plenty for Britain, Sweden, etc. and if you need filler, I'd rather see reprints from "Chariots of Fire" [newsletter for the Washington Interscholastic O League, editor: Dan Waugh, COC]; local orienteers can relate better to local parks and maps. Harvey Friedman COC President

Everyone down here was very pleased with the quality of the first issue and hope that you can make its production easy enough that we can continue to use it as a regional newsletter. I heard that some peolpe were a little upset about the late distribution of the last issue, but really the quality was so much much better than the previous Bearing 315's and other newsletters....well, just keep up the good work. Jeff Lewis, OSUOC

Loved the magazine. It makes us look organized. I think the "Review & Preview" section and the "Club News" section kind of overlap. Kind of think Mike Schuh's challenge cup will be pretty uneven, due to club size (COC vs OABC vs everyone else all together?). Good basic idea though. An idea - get some "old timer" to do a history of NW Orienteering - a series maybe? First meet, first map, etc. Well, we will talk to you again soon. Your Eastern Slope Correspondent, Dale Width, EOC

The Northwest Orienteer is superb!!! Thank you for sending a copy. Some day, I'll get back to your letter to continue our dialogue re: regional organization, regional newsletters, etc. However, your publication speaks volumes on that issue! Congratuiations! Barb Sleight Syracuse OC, NY

Your premier issue of the NW Orienteer was marvelous!! Congratulations - and thanks a million for the time and effort you are putting in. I was particularly interested in the article by Lorrie Bouchard on teaching orienteering to children. We hope to get Day Camp courses made up for G.S. Brownies and Club Scout camps this summer. Addie Graaf, EOC

Congratulations on the publication of the first issue of THE NORTHWEST ORIENTEER ! I enjoyed reading it, and the issue obviously was noticed at the national level as well (ie. the recent praise in OUSA). The contribution of articles and other material by the northwest clubs will ensure the continued success of the publication. We have taken a big step forward toward closer communication and cooperation among the clubs in the region. Dave Enger, SOC

With the IOF and COF working to have Ski-O as a demonstration sport in the 1988 Winter Olympics in Calgary, Canada, hosting the 1988 World Ski-O Championship is being talked about some in the USA and Canada. Northwest orienteering needs to turn some attention towards the promotion and development of Ski-O, with 1988 as a long range aim.

There are only 20 TRIM (permanent) O' courses in the USA with just one in the northwest at Farrel McWhirter Park in Redmond, Wa. Sweden attracted 95,000 new people to orienteering last year through TRIM-O courses - does that suggest a successful way to promote orienteering?

OSU HORSE CENTER 29 JAN Partly sunny skies and the pristine forests behind the OSU Horse Center were enjoyed by numerous people from throughout western Oregon at the OSU Orienteering Club's second meet of the year. Jeff Lewis set three courses for varying abilities on the new 1:10000 map. On the orange course (4.7km + 740 ft, 9 controls) an error was unfortunately made in drafting the master map, so the results had to be thrown out. Nonetheless, times recorded were: Dave Kingham (55:59), Mal Harding (56:07), Chris Loftus (63:07), Wayne Patterson (75:40), and Gordon Haller (110:30). Two recreational entries were Monique Slipher and Oliver Wyrtki. The yellow course (3.0km + 400 ft, 9 controls) had no competitive entries. Recreational entries included: Craig Boyak, Brock Krizan, Dan Pittman, Tom Edsall, Pat Forgey, Jason Mershon, Paul Knutzen, Lynn Baker, and Eve Lonnquist. Only one person, Susan Fay, tried the white course (1.3km + 120 ft of climb, with 7 controls).
John Sampson and Scott Calhoun on finish run-in at a Lincoln Park meet. [Photo]

CHIP ROSS PARK 15 JAN Chip Ross Park and the hills north of Corvallis was the site of the first meet of the year hosted by the OSU Orienteering Club. A light fog prevailed making the terrain all the more interesting to orienteer through. Twenty-eight participants from Portland and the lower Willamette Valley enjoyed courses set by Jeff Lewis on the new 1:15000 map. Four courses were set having the following specifications: WHITE (15): 2.4km + 280ft, 7 controls YELLOW (7): 3.4km + 460ft, 7 controls ORANGE (0): 5.4km + 800ft, 9 controls RED (6): 7.5km + 1200ft, 9 controls (number of entries in parentheses) While many of the participants at OSUOC meets are strictly recreational orienteers, there were a few competitive performances. On the white course, Tom & Hope Pettit (42:00) placed first followed by Pat Forgey (45:00) and Charles and Ryan Fisher (45:50). On the yellow course, club advisor and OSU Geography Professor Jon Kimerling applied his cartographic intuition to win over Neal Jaarsma (76:00) and Pat Faretto (104:15). The red course was won by a British orienteer down from Portland, Dave Kingham (60:30). Wayne Patterson (70:50) had one of his best performances to date. Third place went to Gregg Ferry (89:59). Fourth place went to Chris Loftus (127:53), an experienced orienteer from St. Louis who saw quite alot of Corvallis... ...even some areas not on the map! Pete Kehoe assisted in meet administration. The only negative aspect of the meet was the theft of two controls. Their loss may explain the two DNF's on yellow and one DNF on white.

BEAVER MARSH 13 FEB A new 1:10000 map and springlike weather were the highlights of this Sunday meet sponsored by OSUOC. Low clouds threatened to bring rain, but actual showers were few and much of the time was spent under warm sunshine. Participants from throughout the mid-Willamette Valley were evenly divided among three courses set by Jeff Lewis. Many of the entries were novices on their first or second course; all of them enjoyed the rustic timber/farm lands, abundant wildlife, and sweeping rainbows. The orange course (6.2 km + 700 ft climb, 10 controls) was won by Wayne Patterson, an OSU student, with the time of 60:06. Other competitors included Pete Kehoe (74:01), Francois Colomb (134:40), and Lee Welch (138:44). Scott Linn was the sole recreational entry. The yellow course (4.2 km + 400 ft climb, 6 controls) was also won by an OSU student, Paul Knutzen (90:44). Recreational entries included Neal Jaarsma, Jeff Link, Eve Lonnquist, and Steve Marlatt. The only competitive entry on the white course (2.8 km + 200 ft climb, 8 controls) was David & Lullu Truitt of Salem (101:54). Recreational entries included Harry Bell, David Clifton-Smith, Leslie Simon, and Joe Stevens.
